content:
Ethics in Data Collection and Analysis

In data science, it’s easy to focus on numbers and results, but behind every dataset are real people who trust us with their information. How that information is collected and used can have important ethical implications.

Most of us have completed a survey after finishing a course, training session, or workshop. These surveys are often presented as a way for organizations to gather feedback for improvements. In this scenario, participants are asked to complete a ten-question survey after finishing a course. To access the survey, they must log in using their organization username and password. Although the survey does not ask for their name, it states that all responses will remain anonymous. Executive management plans to use the survey results to identify courses that need improvement, and data analysts are responsible for analyzing the feedback and providing recommendations.

While this survey may seem straightforward, it raises several ethical concerns related to the collection of data and the responsible use of it.

Ethical Issues in the Survey Design and Sample Question

1. False Anonymous Claim

The first ethical concern that stands out is anonymity. The survey claims that responses are anonymous, but participants are required to log in with their organizational account. Even if the survey itself does not ask for a person’s name, the system still records login credentials. Because of this, participants may not truly be anonymous.

This leads to another important ethical concept: transparency. Participants are not given a clear explanation of how privacy is protected. Organizations have a responsibility to be clear about how data is collected and used. If participants believe their responses cannot be linked to them, but the system makes that possible, the survey is not fully transparent. People should know exactly what information is being collected before they agree to participate.

2. Lack of Informed Consent

The issue of informed consent is also relevant. Informed consent means that individuals understand:

  • What data is being collected
  • How it will be used
  • Who will see it
  • Whether it can be linked back to them

The survey description does not explain any of this. Simply stating “anonymous” is not enough. Without clear information, participants cannot make an informed decision about whether to participate.

3. Survey Question Design

The sample survey question raises concerns as well. At first glance, it seems reasonable. However, it combines all course objectives into a single rating. A participant may feel that some objectives were achieved while others were not. By asking about all objectives together, the survey may not capture an accurate picture of the course’s strengths and weaknesses.

Additionally, not every participant may clearly remember all aspects of the course. This could affect the quality and reliability of the data collected.

Other Ethical Issues That Could Be Present

1. Data Privacy and Security

One major issue is where the data is stored, whether it is encrypted, and who has access to it. Without clear policies, there is a risk of violating data privacy and security. Survey responses may contain opinions, criticisms, or comments that participants expect to remain confidential. If the organization does not properly secure this information, unauthorized individuals could gain access. Protecting collected data is an important ethical responsibility.

2. Limited Time Window

The survey is available for a short time. This may systematically exclude groups. Participants who are unavailable during that time—busy, traveling, or managing personal responsibilities—may not get the chance to respond, making the results unbalanced and unfair.

3. Biased Response

Another issue is bias. If participants alter their responses because they fear being identified, the data will not accurately reflect their true opinions. This is known as response bias. As a result, management may make decisions based on misleading information rather than genuine feedback.

4. Misuse of Data

Another possible concern is the future use of the data. The survey is intended to improve courses, but what if management uses the survey results to evaluate instructors or departments? This could cause harm. Surveys intended for improvement should not become tools for scrutiny.

Recommendations for Executive Management

1. Remove Login Requirement

If the survey is anonymous, the system must not require personal credentials. One option would be to separate the login from the survey. Other alternatives include:

  • Anonymous tokens
  • Open access links
  • Temporary survey IDs

2. Provide Clear Informed Consent

The organization should clearly explain how the survey works. Participants should know what information is collected, whether login data is stored, who has access to the responses, and how the results will be used. Greater transparency helps build trust and supports informed consent.

3. Improve Question Design

Survey questions should be clear, neutral, and free from absolute language. Instead of asking participants whether the course met all objectives in a single question, each objective could be evaluated separately. This would provide more detailed and actionable feedback for course improvement.

4. Establish Ethical Governance

The organization should establish strong data governance practices. Access to survey data should be limited to authorized personnel, and the information should be stored securely. Clear policies should define how long the data will be retained and how it may be used in the future.

What If Management Rejects the Recommendations?

As a data analyst, ethical responsibilities do not disappear simply because management disagrees with the concerns raised.

1. Document My Concerns

I would create and maintain a written record of identified risks and recommendations. This demonstrates professional accountability and ensures that concerns have been properly communicated. It also protects me and shows due diligence.

2. Limit My Analysis to Ethical Boundaries

If required to deliver analysis, I would include clear warnings and limitations. I would explain that the findings are based on a survey that may not be truly anonymous and had a limited participation window. This ensures decision-makers understand that the conclusions should be treated as directional, not definitive.

3. Encourage Ethical Data Use

I would recommend using survey results strictly for course improvement and avoiding disciplinary interpretations. This approach reflects the principle of beneficence, which emphasizes doing good while preventing potential harm.

I would not simply go along with the process and pretend there are no problems. If I believe there are concerns about privacy, anonymity, or data quality, I should be honest about them. As a data analyst, it is important to act responsibly, respect people’s information, and be transparent about any limitations in the data.

Conclusion

This survey shows how easily ethical issues can hide beneath a simple form. What looks straightforward actually raises concerns about false anonymity, pressured participation, unclear questions, and sampling bias.

This scenario highlights five key ethical concepts: informed consent, data privacy, transparency, sampling bias, and accountability.

As data professionals, we are not just analyzing numbers; we are responsible for handling information that belongs to real people. When a system fails ethically, it’s our job to point out the issues, advocate for better practices, and refuse to contribute to harm—even when leadership disagrees. The survey can be fixed, but if leadership ignores the issues, the analyst must make the ethical concerns impossible to ignore.
title: The Illusion of Anonymity: When Data Science Ethics Gets Lost in the Survey

content:
Artificial intelligence is now part of almost every area of life. It helps doctors read medical scans, helps banks detect fraud, and even helps governments make decisions about public services. But as AI becomes more powerful, one question becomes more important than ever:

Do we understand how AI makes its decisions?

This question leads us to one of the most important ethical issues in modern technology: transparency and explainability in AI systems.

This blog explores why transparency and explainability matter, how they connect to major AI ethics themes, and what real-world examples teach us.

The Black Box

Although AI offers many benefits, it also raises important ethical questions. One of the biggest concerns is that many AI systems work like a “black box.” They produce answers or decisions, but people often do not know how those decisions were made—even the engineers who built the system.

This lack of understanding creates problems when AI makes mistakes or unfair decisions.

The AI might be weighing hundreds of variables in ways no one fully understands. This matters because AI is increasingly making real decisions about real people. A system that cannot explain itself gives people no way to challenge a bad decision or detect when something is going wrong. And when errors happen—which they do—it becomes very hard to figure out what went wrong and how to fix it.

What Is Transparency in AI?

Transparency means being open about how an AI system works. It includes information about the data used to train the model, how decisions are made, and what the system is designed to do.

When we talk about transparency in AI, we mean two things:

  • Explainability – Can AI provide understandable reasons for its decisions in a way humans can understand?
  • Interpretability – Can experts look inside the system and understand how it actually works?

Transparency is the broader principle of being open about how an AI system works. Explainability is one part of transparency because it helps users understand why the AI reached a particular decision.

For example, imagine a bank uses AI to approve or reject loan applications.

Without explainability, the system simply says:

“Loan application rejected.”

With explainability, it might say:

“The application was rejected because the applicant’s debt-to-income ratio is above the acceptable limit and the credit history shows several missed payments.”

The second response allows people to understand the decision and, if necessary, challenge it.

At first glance, transparency and explainability may seem like technical concepts, but they are also ethical principles because they affect whether people are treated fairly and whether AI decisions can be questioned.

Why Transparency and Explainability Matter?

AI systems influence decisions that affect people’s lives. These decisions include:

  • Who gets hired
  • Who gets a loan
  • Which patient receives urgent care
  • How long someone stays in jail
  • What information people see online

When decisions have real consequences, opacity becomes an ethical problem.

Explainability helps answer questions such as:

  • Why was my job application rejected?
  • Why did the AI think this patient is low risk?
  • Why did the system deny my loan?

Without answers, people feel powerless.

With answers, they can challenge mistakes, correct unfairness, and trust the system.

The importance of transparency becomes even clearer when we look at real-world examples where AI systems have influenced people’s lives.

A Real-World Example: The COMPAS Algorithm

COMPAS (Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions) is a tool used in American courts to help judges decide matters such as bail and sentencing. It gives defendants a score based on how likely they are to reoffend.

Sounds helpful, but there was a big problem: nobody could see how the system reached its conclusions.

In 2016, journalists at ProPublica found that the system was treating people unfairly. Black defendants were almost twice as likely to be wrongly labelled as high risk compared to white defendants in similar situations.

However, there was one major problem: nobody could clearly understand how the algorithm reached its decision. The company that built COMPAS refused to share how it worked, calling it a trade secret.

In Wisconsin v. Loomis (2016), a defendant challenged this in court, arguing it was unfair to be sentenced based on a score that nobody could examine. The court still ruled against him.

That means someone could go to prison partly because of an algorithm that no one was allowed to question.

That is not just a technical flaw—it is an ethical failure.

Core Principles of AI Ethics

The COMPAS case demonstrates why transparency is closely connected to several core principles of AI ethics.

1. Fairness

The COMPAS algorithm failed the fairness principle because it produced higher risk scores for Black defendants compared to white defendants with similar records. A fair system should treat everyone equally, regardless of race or background.

2. Accountability

Accountability requires that someone is responsible for the decisions an AI system influences. With COMPAS, judges relied on the scores, but the company refused to reveal how the algorithm worked. Without transparency, accountability becomes almost impossible.

3. Transparency

Transparency means people should be able to understand how decisions are made. COMPAS was a black box, and defendants could not challenge or question their risk scores because the company kept the model secret.

4. Non-Maleficence (Do No Harm)

The algorithm caused real harm by labelling some defendants as high risk when they were not. This led to longer sentences, fewer opportunities for parole, and lasting personal consequences.

5. Respect for Human Dignity

Every person deserves to be treated with dignity and respect. When an AI system assigns a risk score without explanation and influences a person’s freedom, it reduces them to a number. COMPAS did not allow defendants to understand or challenge the score, undermining their dignity and right to a fair process.

Transparency and AI Governance

Because of cases like COMPAS and recognizing these risks, governments and international organizations have introduced AI governance rules that promote transparency and accountability and guide how AI systems are developed and used.

The European Union’s AI Act, which came into force in 2024, is one of the strongest examples. It requires that AI systems used in sensitive areas—such as hiring, education, credit decisions, and law enforcement—must be transparent and explainable. People affected by these systems have the right to know how a decision was made.

Similarly, the OECD AI Principles, adopted by more than 40 countries, identify transparency and explainability as core requirements for responsible AI development.

These frameworks show a growing international consensus:

Explainability is not optional—it is a basic requirement.

How Ethical Principles Support Transparent and Explainable AI?

Transparency and explainability are essential for building ethical AI systems.

Ethical AI frameworks encourage developers to create AI that is fair, accountable, and transparent so users can understand how decisions are made.

AI governance supports these principles by establishing policies and oversight to ensure AI systems are monitored, tested for bias, and used responsibly.

Soft ethics goes beyond legal requirements by encouraging organizations to be open about how AI works and how data is used, even when the law does not require it.

At the same time, applying data science ethics to AI ensures that data is collected and used fairly, privacy is respected, and biased outcomes are reduced.

Together, these principles help make AI systems more transparent, explainable, and trustworthy for everyone.

Conclusion: A Future Where AI Speaks Clearly

The COMPAS story is a powerful reminder that AI transparency is not just a nice feature—it is an ethical necessity.

When AI systems make consequential decisions without explanation, bias goes unchecked, people lose their ability to defend themselves, and trust breaks down.

The good news is that we are not powerless.

AI governance frameworks like the EU AI Act are setting new standards. Researchers are developing explainable AI tools. And a growing number of organizations are embracing the spirit of soft ethics, doing more than the law requires because they genuinely care about getting it right.

But in the end, it comes down to a simple principle:

If an AI system is making decisions that affect real people, it should be able to explain itself.

That is not too much to ask. In fact, it is the very least we should expect.

If AI cannot explain its decisions, it becomes difficult to trust its judgments.

Transparency is therefore not just a technical feature; it is the foundation of ethical and trustworthy AI.

References

Transparency & Explainability

European Commission. Guidelines for Providers and Deployers of High-Risk AI Systems. https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/guidelines-ai-high-risk-systems

White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights. https://regulations.ai/regulations/united-states-2022-10-ai-bill-of-rights

Criminal Justice Algorithms

ProPublica. (2016). Machine Bias: There’s Software Used Across the Country to Predict Future Criminals. And It’s Biased Against Blacks. https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing

AI Governance & Ethics

European Parliament. (2024). EU AI Act: First Regulation on Artificial Intelligence. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/topics/en/article/20230601STO93804/eu-ai-act-first-regulation-on-artificial-intelligence

OECD. (2019). Recommendation of the Council on Artificial Intelligence. https://oecd.ai/en/ai-principles
description: Why transparency and explainability are essential for building ethical, trustworthy AI systems, with lessons from the COMPAS algorithm and emerging AI governance frameworks.
title: If AI Can't Explain Itself, Should We Trust It?

content:
Transition Into Analytics

SQL is one of the most important skills for data analysts. Whether you’re cleaning data, generating reports, or answering business questions, SQL helps transform raw data into meaningful insights.

As I transitioned into data analytics, SQL became one of the first technical skills I focused on learning. While SQL contains many advanced features, there are a few fundamental queries that analysts use regularly in real-world projects.

In this post, I’ll cover five SQL queries every data analyst should know and explain why they are important.

1. Filtering Data with WHERE

The WHERE clause allows analysts to retrieve only the data relevant to a specific question.

Example
SELECT *
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Sales';
Why It Matters

In real-world datasets, analysts rarely need every record. Filtering helps focus on a specific customer segment, product category, region, or time period.

Common use cases include:

  • Finding customers from a specific city
  • Analyzing a particular product line
  • Reviewing transactions within a date range

2. Summarizing Data with GROUP BY

The GROUP BY clause is used to aggregate data and calculate metrics such as totals, averages, and counts.

Example
SELECT department,
       COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
Why It Matters

Business leaders often need summarized information rather than individual records.

Examples include:

  • Total sales by region
  • Number of customers by country
  • Average salary by department

Grouping data helps reveal patterns that are difficult to identify from raw rows alone.

3. Combining Tables with JOIN

Data is often stored across multiple tables. The JOIN statement allows analysts to combine related information.

Example
SELECT c.customer_name,
       o.order_date,
       o.order_amount
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;
Why It Matters

Most business databases are relational.

A typical analysis may require combining:

  • Customers and Orders
  • Employees and Departments
  • Products and Sales

Understanding joins is essential because valuable insights often exist across multiple datasets rather than within a single table.

4. Finding Top Results with ORDER BY and LIMIT

Analysts frequently need to identify the highest or lowest values in a dataset.

Example
SELECT product_name,
       total_sales
FROM products
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 5;
Why It Matters

This query helps answer questions such as:

  • What are the top-selling products?
  • Which customers generate the most revenue?
  • Which regions have the highest sales?

Ranking data is a common requirement in dashboards and management reports.

5. Using Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions help summarize large amounts of data quickly.

Example
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    SUM(order_amount) AS total_revenue,
    AVG(order_amount) AS average_order_value
FROM orders;
Common Aggregate Functions
Function Purpose
COUNT() Counts records
SUM() Calculates totals
AVG() Calculates averages
MIN() Finds lowest value
MAX() Finds highest value
Why It Matters

Organizations rely on KPIs and summary metrics to make decisions. Aggregate functions provide the foundation for many business reports and dashboards.

Putting It All Together

In practice, analysts often combine these concepts.

Example
SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS customers,
       SUM(sales_amount) AS total_sales
FROM sales
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;

This single query:

  • Filters data
  • Groups records
  • Calculates totals
  • Sorts results

These are the exact skills analysts use daily when working with business data.

Key Takeaways

The five SQL concepts every data analyst should master are:

  1. WHERE – Filter data
  2. GROUP BY – Summarize data
  3. JOIN – Combine tables
  4. ORDER BY and LIMIT – Rank results
  5. Aggregate Functions – Calculate KPIs

While SQL offers many advanced capabilities, these fundamentals form the foundation of effective data analysis. Mastering them will make it easier to explore data, answer business questions, and build meaningful insights.

Conclusion

Learning SQL has been an important part of my transition into data analytics. The more I work with data, the more I appreciate how powerful SQL is for extracting information and supporting data-driven decisions.

For aspiring analysts, focusing on these five core query types is a great starting point. Once these concepts become second nature, you’ll be well prepared to tackle more advanced analytical challenges.
description: Five essential SQL queries that every aspiring data analyst should understand and use regularly.
title: 5 SQL Queries Every Data Analyst Should Know

content:
First Step Towards Visualization

Creating my first Power BI dashboard was an exciting milestone in my data analytics journey. It helped me turn raw data into meaningful insights and taught me how powerful visualization can be in storytelling. Building this dashboard was an eye‑opening experience into how global economic shifts impact companies, industries, and entire regions.

1. Project Overview

I built a World Layoffs - Insignts & Trends dashboard to analyze worldwide layoffs from 2020 to 2024, covering more than 1,000 companies, 31 industries, and nearly 485,000 employees affected. The goal of this project was to understand how layoffs evolved globally during and after the pandemic. I wanted to explore:

  • Which companies had the highest layoffs
  • Which industries were most affected
  • How layoffs changed year‑over‑year
  • Which regions experienced the largest impact
  • Monthly patterns and deep‑dive trends

Tool Used: Power BI Desktop
Dataset: Global Layoffs (2020–2024) — includes company details, industry, country, layoff counts, percentage laid off, funds raised, and timeline fields

2. Data Preparation

Before creating visuals, I performed data cleaning and transformation using Power Query Editor, ensuring accuracy and consistancy before modeling.

Key Steps Included:

  • Removing duplicates and null values
  • Standardizing industry and country names
  • Converting date fields into proper formats
  • Creating Year, Month, and Quarter columns
  • Adding calculated fields for YoY change and total layoffs
  • Ensuring numeric fields (layoff count, funds raised) were properly typed

3. Dashboard Design

I designed a two‑page dashboard to separate high‑level insights from deeper analysis.

Page 1: Global Layoff Overview

Focused on global patterns and top‑level metrics.

Key visuals:

  • KPI Cards: Total layoffs, average layoff %, max single-company layoff
  • Top 10 Companies by Layoffs
  • Industry‑Wise Layoffs (Pie Chart)
  • Yearly Totals + YoY Change
  • Global Map (Layoffs by Country)

Page 2: Detailed Layoff Insights & Deep Dive

This page explores detailed patterns and company‑level insights.

Key visuals:

  • Companies with 100% Layoffs (shutdowns)
  • Layoffs by Country & Industry (stacked bar)
  • Monthly Layoffs Trend (2020–2024)

My goal was a clean, intuitive layout that allows users to move from a global view to detailed insights seamlessly.

4. Key Insights

Tech and Retail were the hardest hit

Tech companies like Amazon, Tesla, Dell, Google, Meta, and SAP dominated the Top 10 list. Retail and Transportation also showed significant layoffs due to shifting consumer behavior and supply chain disruptions.

Layoffs peaked in 2023

The YoY chart revealed a sharp spike in 2023 — the highest in the dataset. 2024 showed early signs of stabilization.

Several companies shut down completely

The “100% Layoffs” table highlighted companies that ceased operations, including:

  • Katerra (Construction)
  • Bitwise (Tech)
  • Zulily (Retail)
  • Convoy (Logistics)

These cases often involved startups with high fundraising but unsustainable cost structures.

North America and Europe saw the largest impact

The map visualization showed the highest concentration of layoffs in the US, Canada, UK, and Germany.

Monthly trends showed seasonal patterns

Layoffs often spiked in Q1, aligning with annual restructuring cycles.

5. Challenges Faced

Like any real‑world project, this one came with its own learning curve:

  • Understanding how to model multiple dimensions (Company, Industry, Country, Date)
  • Choosing visuals that clearly communicate trends
  • Designing a layout that works across two pages
  • Syncing slicers across pages for a smooth user experience
  • Ensuring the dashboard remained clean despite large amounts of data

Each challenge helped me improve my BI design and analytical thinking.

6. What I Learned

This project strengthened my skills in:

  • Data cleaning and transformation using Power Query
  • Data modeling with a star schema
  • DAX calculations for YoY metrics and KPIs
  • Dashboard storytelling — turning raw data into meaningful insights
  • Designing multi‑page Power BI reports
  • Creating interactive visuals that support deeper exploration

Most importantly, I learned how to structure a dashboard that feels professional, intuitive, and portfolio‑ready.

Final Thoughts

The World Layoffs dashboard helped me understand how global events ripple across industries and regions. It also allowed me to demonstrate my ability to work with complex datasets, build insightful visuals, and communicate findings clearly.

This project reflects my passion for data storytelling and my commitment to building dashboards that deliver real value.
title: Building My First Power BI Dashboard

content:
Introduction

My journey into data analytics began while working in pension administration, where I was responsible for financial data reconciliation, reporting, and validating pension payment records. Although accuracy and compliance were key aspects of the role, I found myself particularly interested in analyzing data, identifying discrepancies, and uncovering patterns.

Discovering Analytics

A significant part of my work involved comparing datasets from banks against internal records, performing variance analysis in Excel, and preparing reports for management. These responsibilities strengthened my analytical thinking and attention to detail while showing me the value of data-driven decision-making.

Over time, I realized I wanted to deepen my analytical skills and work with more advanced tools that could transform raw data into actionable insights.

Building Technical Skills

To support this transition, I began learning:

  • SQL and MySQL for data querying and database management
  • Power BI for dashboard development and business reporting
  • Tableau for interactive data visualization
  • Advanced Excel for reporting and data manipulation
  • Python for data analysis and automation

I also started working on hands-on projects using real-world datasets to strengthen my practical experience.

Current Learning Path

I am currently pursuing a Diploma in Data Analytics & AI Specialist, where I continue to expand my knowledge of analytics, business intelligence, and emerging AI technologies.

Looking Ahead

My goal is to combine my business experience and analytical skills to help organizations make informed decisions through data. As I continue learning and building projects, I look forward to applying data analytics to solve real-world business challenges and create meaningful impact.
description: How my experience in pension administration inspired my transition into data analytics."
title: My Journey into Data Analytics

Hugo ships with several Embedded Shortcodes for rich content, along with a Privacy Config and a set of Simple Shortcodes that enable static and no-JS versions of various social media embeds.

X Simple Shortcode

{{< x user="GoHugoIO" id="1315233626070503424" >}}

Profile is a customizable single-page portfolio theme for Hugo featuring a contact form and blog. Made by @gurusabarishh.https://t.co/KwYcwfdy16

— GoHugo.io (@GoHugoIO) October 11, 2020

Vimeo Simple Shortcode

{{< vimeo 146022717 >}}


Youtube Simple Shortcode

{{< youtube w7Ft2ymGmfc >}}


Theme Custom Shortcodes

These shortcodes are not Hugo built-ins, but are provided by the theme.

Responsive Images with Cloudinary

You can learn more about this here.

Set the cloudinary_cloud_name parameter in your site config to use this shortcode.

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" >}}

Note that you do not include the file extension (e.g. .png) in the src parameter, as the shortcode will automatically determine the best quality and format for the user’s device.

Optionally, you can customize the general CSS styles for the image:

{{< dynamic-img src="/my/image/on/cloudinary" title="A title for the image" style="max-width:60%" >}}
title: Rich Content
